God will not allow his people to remain inactive in sin. By this parable Nathan drew a sentence against himself from David. There is great need for prudence in giving corrections. In his application, he was faithful. He says plainly, “You are the man.” God shows how much he hates sin, even in his own people; and wherever he finds it, he will not let it go unpunished. David does not say a word to excuse himself or make light of his sin, but freely acknowledges it. When David said, "I have sinned," and Nathan perceived that he was a true penitent, he assured him that his sin was forgiven. You will not die; that is, not die eternally, nor be forever put away from God, as you would be if you had not removed the sin. Though you will all your days be chastened by the Lord, yet you will not be condemned with the world. There is this great evil in the sins of those who profess religion and relationship to God, that they furnish the enemies of God and religion with matter for reproach and blasphemy. And it appears from David's case that even where pardon is obtained, the Lord will visit the transgression of his people with the rod, and their iniquity with stripes. For one momentary gratification of a vile lust, David had to endure many days and years of extreme distress.

David now penned Psalm 51, in which, though he had been assured that his sin was forgiven, he prays earnestly for forgiveness and greatly laments his sin. He was willing to bear the shame of it, to have it ever before him, to be continually reminded of it. God gives us leave to be earnest with him in prayer for particular blessings, from trust in his power and general mercy, though we have no particular promise to build upon. David patiently submitted to the will of God in the death of one child, and God made up the loss to his advantage, in the birth of another. The way to have creature comforts continued or restored, or the loss made up some other way, is cheerfully to resign them to God. God, by his grace, particularly owned and favored that son, and ordered him to be called Jedidiah, Beloved of the Lord. Our prayers for our children are graciously and as fully answered when some of them die in their infancy, for they are well taken care of, and when others live, “beloved of the Lord.”

To be so harsh in enslaving the children of Ammon was a sign that David’s heart had not yet been softened by repentance at the time this occurred. We will be most compassionate, kind, and forgiving to others when we most feel our need for the Lord's forgiving love and taste its sweetness in our own souls.
